ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bun Bo Hue. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent; no specific Bun Bo Hue code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Bun Bo Hue or a sufficiently close Vietnamese noodle soup concept.

allergens: gluten risk from soy sauce variants

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bun Bo Hue Dac Biet weighing approximately 550 g, comprising rice vermicelli noodles, broth, pork leg, beef shin, beef tendon, and pork ham.

nutrient_source.title: Bun Bo Hue (Vietnamese Spicy Beef and Pork Noodle Soup) — nutrient profile estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese noodle soups and USDA FoodData Central component data for pork leg, beef shin, beef tendon, and rice vermicelli
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bun Bo Hue Dac Biet. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: rice vermicelli (~40% by weight), mixed pork and beef cuts (~30%), broth (~25%), and condiments/vegetables (~5%). Reference anchors: FAO/INFOODS SEA Vietnamese noodle soup entries and USDA SR Legacy entries for pork leg (cooked), beef shank (cooked), beef tendon (cooked), and rice noodles (cooked). Values represent a plausible mid-range estimate for a restaurant-prepared special bowl.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles and mixed noodle soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bun Bo Hue. GI estimated at 53 based on the GI of cooked rice vermicelli noodles (GI ~53, low category) as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice noodles and mixed Asian noodle soups. The protein and fat content of the meat components further attenuate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 46.75) / 100 ≈ 24.8, rounded to 25.0 g (serving size 550 g, carbohydrate 8.5 g/100 g × 5.5 = 46.75 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from bone broth, fish sauce, and shrimp paste
hypertension.guidance: A single 550 g bowl provides approximately 2310 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth and limit portion size or frequency of consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli noodles, partially offset by protein and fat from meat components
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 25 per bowl is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of noodles, consider requesting a smaller noodle portion with extra protein, and account for this meal within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from pork leg and beef cuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~2310 mg per bowl) and saturated fat from pork leg and beef shin warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing leaner cuts, reducing broth intake, and limiting frequency is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bun Bo Hue Dac Biet (Bún Bò Huế Đặc Biệt) is a special version of the classic Vietnamese spicy beef and pork noodle soup originating from Hue, the former imperial capital of Vietnam. Unlike the more internationally familiar pho, Bun Bo Hue is characterised by its bold, spicy, and lemongrass-infused broth made from slow-simmered beef and pork bones, seasoned with fermented shrimp paste (mam ruoc), fish sauce, and fresh chilli. The 'Dac Biet' (special) designation indicates the inclusion of premium toppings: pork leg (chan gio), beef shin (bap bo), beef tendon (gan bo), and Vietnamese pork ham (cha lua). The noodles used are round, thick rice vermicelli (bun), which have a slightly chewy texture distinct from the flat rice noodles of pho. The dish is served with a garnish platter of fresh herbs, bean sprouts, banana blossom, and lime. Nutritionally, a standard bowl provides approximately 78 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~6.2 g/100 g) from the mixed meat components and moderate carbohydrate (~8.5 g/100 g) from the rice noodles. Sodium content is high (~420 mg/100 g, ~2310 mg per bowl) due to the seasoned broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category) based on rice vermicelli, with a moderate glycemic load per serving. Clinically, portion control of noodles and broth is recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Bun Bo Hue Dac Biet as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy data for individual ingredients. GI is estimated by analogy from rice vermicelli GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no single approved-source entry for Bun Bo Hue Dac Biet exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a named composite dish. Values should be validated against a laboratory analysis or a future HPB Singapore entry if one becomes available. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this estimation. 3. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA component entries. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in naturally prepared pork and beef cuts per USDA SR Legacy data. 8. The dish is classified as Vietnamese cuisine despite being served in Singapore by a Vietnamese restaurant; the cuisine tag reflects culinary origin. 9. Halal status is false as the dish contains pork. 10. GI estimated at 53 based on rice vermicelli analogy from University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per serving ~25.0 g for a 550 g bowl.
